title: Staff Infection

jokes.text: So this isn't bad, right? just like a normal Saturday. You don't have to drive so slow. I can ride my bike by myself. This actually doesn't go any faster.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to have quality father-son time while working by driving alongside George Michael's bike
jokes.punchline: Michael's car is stuck in traffic/going so slow it can't go faster than a bike
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual comedy setup - the absurdity of a 'bike ride' where dad drives alongside. The punchline that the car literally can't go faster subverts expectations beautifully.

jokes.text: Nice instincts, son. Good reflexes.
jokes.setup: George Michael presumably crashes or has an accident while Michael is on the phone
jokes.punchline: Michael's sarcastic praise after clearly causing the accident by being distracted
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael deflection humor - turning his own negligence into praise for his son. Economy and character integration are strong.

jokes.text: I ain't going to squeal, man. I ain't going to... Oh, I'm not frightened! God!
jokes.setup: Tobias practicing his role as a frightened inmate
jokes.punchline: He breaks character to complain that he's not convincingly frightened
jokes.explanation: Pure Tobias - the actor so bad he can't even convince himself. Character integration is perfect as only Tobias would break character this way.

jokes.text: Lindsay, say something to scare me. ...me. Nope. Nothing. Thanks for trying though.
jokes.setup: Tobias asks Lindsay to help him practice being frightened
jokes.punchline: Lindsay either says something horrible or nothing at all, neither works to frighten him
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered joke about their marriage - either Lindsay says something so cruel it's unsurprising, or she can't be bothered to help. The awkward beat sells it.

jokes.text: You know what I ought to do? I ought to check myself into a men's penal colony. Perhaps your father could get me in.
jokes.setup: Tobias discussing his acting preparation needs
jokes.punchline: Unconscious double entendre with 'men's penal colony' given Tobias's repressed sexuality
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias double entendre. The fact that he's completely oblivious while the audience catches the sexual undertone is perfect character work.

jokes.text: Which isn't a name... but the Korean word for 'hello'.
jokes.setup: Lucille calls her adopted Korean son 'Annyong'
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals she's been calling him 'hello' this whole time
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cultural misunderstanding joke that sets up a running gag. Perfect economy and the narrator's deadpan delivery makes it land perfectly.

jokes.text: Suddenly playing with yourself is a scholarly pursuit.
jokes.setup: Buster claiming he enjoys 'scholarly pursuits'
jokes.punchline: Lucille's cutting response with sexual double meaning
jokes.explanation: Sharp one-liner from Lucille. Perfect economy and the double meaning works on multiple levels - masturbation and literal self-play/narcissism.

jokes.text: Buster had an unfortunate encounter in a photo booth on the wildlife-populated island of Catalina.
jokes.setup: Mention of Catalina triggers explanation of why Buster can't go
jokes.punchline: Vague reference to some traumatic wildlife encounter in a photo booth
jokes.explanation: Classic AD mysterious backstory joke. The specificity of 'photo booth' makes it funnier and more absurd.

jokes.text: We can't have them hanging around like freeloaders, looking for an easy buck.
jokes.setup: Lucille complaining about Lupe's family using their parking lot
jokes.punchline: She's literally describing herself - a freeloader looking for easy money
jokes.explanation: Perfect character irony - Lucille's complete lack of self-awareness while describing exactly what she does. The hypocrisy is beautifully crafted.

jokes.text: Illusion, Mom. And I'm the president of that company.
jokes.setup: Lucille talking about maintaining her 'lifestyle image'
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects 'image' to 'illusion' and asserts his authority
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to Michael's tendency to correct terminology, plus his exasperation at having no real authority despite his title.

jokes.text: They made me apply for a membership card. It was humiliating.
jokes.setup: Michael told her to shop at discount store Quantity Plus
jokes.punchline: Lucille finds applying for a membership card at a discount store humiliating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille - her privileged perspective where basic consumer experiences are degrading. Character integration is flawless.

jokes.text: Does that mean I have to go to your office to get mine, too, or how's that work?
jokes.setup: Michael cuts off Lucille's paycheck
jokes.punchline: Lindsay reveals she also gets a paycheck from the company
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke - just when you think the paycheck problem is solved, it gets worse. Lindsay's casual delivery sells the absurdity.

jokes.text: Well, I usually ask Kitty, uh, but she's not around... and you're the only one here I've ever seen before.
jokes.setup: Employee asks Lindsay how she can just ask for a paycheck
jokes.punchline: Lindsay admits she doesn't know anyone at her own family's company
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lindsay obliviousness - she's been collecting paychecks while knowing literally no one at the company. The casual admission is beautifully written.

jokes.text: You quit college. Yeah, well, I had a job. What was the point?
jokes.setup: Employee mentions being hired out of college
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's backwards logic that having a job negates the need for education
jokes.explanation: Classic privileged logic flip - Lindsay's reasoning is so backwards it's brilliant. Shows her complete disconnection from how normal employment works.

jokes.text: Michael, it's Friday. Everybody coasts on Friday. It's actually Saturday.
jokes.setup: Lindsay saying everyone coasts on Friday
jokes.punchline: Employee points out it's actually Saturday, making it even worse
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation - Lindsay's work ethic is even worse than we thought. The correction makes her laziness more absurd.

jokes.text: Uh, Lindsay. Hello. The phones. Well, you said 'staff.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Lindsay to answer phones instead of attending staff meeting
jokes.punchline: Lindsay interprets 'staff meeting' literally to avoid work
jokes.explanation: Smart wordplay joke - Lindsay finds any excuse to avoid responsibility. The economy is perfect and the logic is beautifully stupid.

jokes.text: What's next, Michael? Are you going to make dancing illegal? Is this the tiny town from Footloose?
jokes.setup: Lindsay complaining about Michael's management style regarding fun at work
jokes.punchline: She compares him to the uptight town in Footloose over fluorescent lights
jokes.explanation: Decent pop culture reference with good escalation, but fairly predictable. The Footloose comparison is apt for Lindsay's dramatic perspective.

jokes.text: Bob? Bob? Bob? Bob? Bob?
jokes.setup: Buster is anxious in the confined copy room looking for Bob who quit
jokes.punchline: Buster's mounting panic as he repeatedly calls for someone who isn't there
jokes.explanation: Simple escalating panic joke. Works for Buster's character but not particularly original. The repetition builds anxiety effectively.

jokes.text: Yeah, I guess. Sorry. Yeah. I'm just bored. That's okay. I guess he just likes work more than he likes you.
jokes.setup: Maeby asking if she's trying to make George Michael feel bad about his dad working
jokes.punchline: Her blunt assessment that Michael likes work more than his son
jokes.explanation: Classic Maeby cruelty delivered matter-of-factly. The uncomfortable truth hits hard and her casual delivery makes it worse.

jokes.text: The warden could easily be black. Oh! Yes.
jokes.setup: Warden mentions wanting to get a script to Carl Weathers
jokes.punchline: Casual suggestion that his character could be race-swapped
jokes.explanation: Absurd Hollywood moment where character race is treated as completely interchangeable. Satirizes colorblind casting discussions.

jokes.text: And I can't put you into 212, because there's already another actor... researching a role in there... Steve Buscemi.
jokes.setup: Warden explaining cell assignments to Tobias
jokes.punchline: Casual mention that Steve Buscemi is also method acting in prison
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist detail - the idea that method actors regularly use actual prison cells for research. Steve Buscemi name-drop works because he's believable in this context.

jokes.text: And I can't put you into 212, because there's already another actor researching a role in there... Steve Buscemi.
jokes.setup: Warden explaining prison cell assignments for Tobias
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of famous actor doing method research
jokes.explanation: Great throwaway celebrity cameo joke that suggests this is normal

jokes.text: You know, that Kitty is starting to sound pretty damn sexy to me. Maybe I oughta... That was Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Gob thinks he's hearing Kitty's sexy voice on the phone
jokes.punchline: Michael reveals it was Lindsay answering
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke with the added layer of Gob being attracted to his sister's voice. The reversal is well-timed.

jokes.text: The guy's like the world's biggest chicken. I'm not a chicken. Yeah, he's not a chicken. He just doesn't like confined spaces, that's all. No, it's both.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Buster being afraid
jokes.punchline: Buster admits he's afraid of both confined AND open spaces
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster logic - he's afraid of literally everything. The escalation from one phobia to covering all spaces is beautifully absurd.

jokes.text: One of the guys told me to take my head out of my bottom and get back to work. My bottom!
jokes.setup: Buster describing the rough language construction workers use
jokes.punchline: He euphemizes 'ass' to 'bottom' and is delighted by the 'roughness'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster - his sheltered life means even mild profanity seems extreme. His excitement about 'bottom' is hilariously innocent.

jokes.text: Hey, pick up the pace, douche bag! Michael! You too, candy ass! 'Candy ass.'
jokes.setup: Construction worker yelling at Michael
jokes.punchline: Buster delightedly repeating 'candy ass' like it's exotic vocabulary
jokes.explanation: Continuation of Buster's fascination with construction site language. His wonder at basic profanity maintains the character perfectly.

jokes.text: What happened to the lights? Isn't this better? Doesn't this just wanna make you curl up and forget about the world?
jokes.setup: Office lights have been turned off
jokes.punchline: Lindsay thinks dark office is cozy instead of impractical for work
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's impractical management style - prioritizing 'vibes' over basic workplace functionality. Her description is absurdly inappropriate for an office.

jokes.text: I told you, these people are sheep. All right? They'll wander off, and you'll lose the sheep. You've lost the sheep.
jokes.setup: Michael warning Lindsay about taking employees to lunch without a plan
jokes.punchline: Immediate confirmation of his prediction as employees wander off
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - Michael's metaphor immediately proves accurate. The repetition of 'sheep' and quick confirmation makes it land well.

jokes.text: In the parking lot, the employees boarded the bus... Lupe had rented for her family reunion... thinking Lindsay had reserved it to take them to lunch.
jokes.setup: Employees leaving for lunch without a plan
jokes.punchline: They accidentally board a bus meant for Lupe's family reunion
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the 'lost sheep' metaphor. The specificity of them boarding the wrong bus for the wrong event is perfectly absurd and well-crafted.

jokes.text: So the employees headed off to Catalina... providing the surveillance team that had installed the lighting... with the opportunity they needed. The place is empty. All right. Let's get in there and bring this company down.
jokes.setup: Employees accidentally leaving on wrong bus
jokes.punchline: FBI agents were waiting for the building to empty so they could investigate
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff revealing the fluorescent lights were FBI surveillance. Multiple storylines converge perfectly with Lindsay's incompetent management accidentally helping federal investigation.

jokes.text: Uh, okay, what say from now on we call you David Ben-Avram? What are you doing to my guy here?
jokes.setup: George Sr. trying to convert inmates to Judaism
jokes.punchline: He's giving obviously Jewish names to white supremacists
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of George Sr.'s prison survival strategy. Converting white supremacists to Judaism is brilliantly absurd and dangerous.

jokes.text: There's the Jewish notion of heaven and that... that it can be attained here on Earth... and there is your belief in the cleansing power of the pipe.
jokes.setup: George Sr. trying to find common ground between Judaism and white supremacy
jokes.punchline: He equates Jewish theology with prison weapon violence
jokes.explanation: Darkly brilliant joke equating religious philosophy with prison violence. George Sr.'s desperate diplomacy creates absurd theological comparisons.

jokes.text: Tobias, what the hell are you doing here? I'm here to study with you, to learn from you. Teach me. There's no teaching. There's no teaching.
jokes.setup: Tobias arrives to study with George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. frantically repeating 'no teaching' to avoid getting in trouble
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the immediate threat. George Sr.'s panic about Tobias wanting to 'learn' creates perfect escalation of his dangerous situation.

jokes.text: And who is this shiny building of a man? Oh, I'm very scared right now.
jokes.setup: Tobias meeting the intimidating inmate
jokes.punchline: His obviously attracted description followed by claiming fear
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tobias double entendre. 'Shiny building of a man' is clearly sexual attraction while he claims to be acting scared. The contrast is beautifully executed.

jokes.text: He no have father? Older lady adopt him too? No, he have father. Father no love him.
jokes.setup: Discussion about George Michael's father not being around
jokes.punchline: Annyong's blunt assessment that the father doesn't love him
jokes.explanation: Dark humor from unexpected source. Annyong's broken English makes the harsh truth hit even harder. The cultural directness contrasts with American politeness.

jokes.text: I went to beach once. Next thing I know, I'm in crate next to pig.
jokes.setup: George Michael mentioning going to the beach
jokes.punchline: Annyong's casual reference to his traumatic immigration experience
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy. Annyong's matter-of-fact delivery of his horrific trafficking experience is perfectly timed and executed. Economy is superb.

jokes.text: Hola. Is Rosa still alive? No? Hmm. Okay. This is not my day.
jokes.setup: Lucille trying to call someone for help
jokes.punchline: Casual reaction to learning someone died, treating it as personal inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille narcissism - someone's death is just an inconvenience to her. The casual 'hmm' and personal frustration are brilliantly callous.

jokes.text: You got paid? I thought we were all making sacrifices. Yeah, well, maybe it's time for some of those big shots at the Bluth Company... to start making some sacrifices.
jokes.setup: Buster discovering Gob got paid while others didn't
jokes.punchline: Worker's ironic comment about 'big shots' to literal Bluth family member
jokes.explanation: Good irony - worker complaining about Bluth Company executives to a Bluth. The dramatic irony works well as audience knows what worker doesn't.

jokes.text: Don't listen to him. He'll never stand up to the man. He's a chicken. A chicken.
jokes.setup: Gob trying to incite workers against Buster
jokes.punchline: Continues chicken running gag about Buster
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to running family joke about Buster being chicken

jokes.text: My whole life you've called me a chicken, but that's over now. I have nothing to prove. That is not how a chicken sounds. Chickens don't clap!
jokes.setup: Buster standing up to Gob calling him a chicken
jokes.punchline: Buster presumably does chicken dance that includes clapping
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - Buster claims he won't prove anything then immediately does elaborate chicken dance. Gob's pedantic correction about chicken sounds is brilliant.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm definitely gonna need a cushion.
jokes.setup: Buster agreeing to dangerous game of chicken with bulldozers
jokes.punchline: His immediate concern about seat comfort rather than mortal danger
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster priorities - worried about comfort in a life-threatening situation. The specificity and character integration are flawless.

jokes.text: Hey, who's ready to have some fun?
jokes.setup: Lindsay trying to solve work stoppage problem
jokes.punchline: She's addressing Lupe's family reunion group as potential replacement workers
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Lindsay's management disasters. Her cheery approach to what's essentially scab labor recruiting is perfectly tone-deaf.

jokes.text: Oh, no. Where is... Where's my phone? I used it to dig the 'B' unit. You what? I'll get it. No, no. You're gonna ruin it! What am I doing?
jokes.setup: Michael needs his phone for important business call
jokes.punchline: He used his phone as a digging tool for sand castle, then panics about ruining it further
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of Michael's work obsession vs. relaxation attempt. Using phone as sand castle tool then panicking about damage perfectly captures his priorities.

jokes.text: Great. Now we're never gonna finish phase three, and everything's gonna fall apart. This whole thing is gonna cave in, and we're gonna be left with a bunch of... Great. Now my sand castle has a giant pool in it.
jokes.setup: Michael ranting about construction project failing
jokes.punchline: Realizes he's describing his collapsed sand castle
jokes.explanation: Brilliant parallel between real construction problems and sand castle - leads to solution

jokes.text: He knew his father had always loved him... but at this moment, he was actually holding the proof in his hands.
jokes.setup: George Michael finding something while his father works on weekend
jokes.punchline: He's holding evidence of his father's love (phone with 'George Michael' sand castle)
jokes.explanation: Beautiful emotional payoff - George Michael discovers his father was making a sand castle of him. The sweetness contrasts perfectly with the chaos around it.

jokes.text: Wow. Who won?
jokes.setup: Lindsay arrives after chicken game between Gob and Buster
jokes.punchline: Both are presumably injured/destroyed equally
jokes.explanation: Visual gag payoff suggesting mutual destruction

jokes.text: I'm a professor of American Studies... at the University of Mexico City. I took a dance class there.
jokes.setup: Lindsay calling the Mexican workers 'laborers'
jokes.punchline: One is actually a professor, Lindsay's only credential is a dance class at same university
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation exposing Lindsay's assumptions and privilege. The professor has more education while she took one dance class - brilliant reversal.

jokes.text: Well, let's discuss this bunking situation. You'll be sleeping under me for a while. I sold you for a pack of cigarettes.
jokes.setup: Prison bunking arrangements being discussed
jokes.punchline: Sexual innuendo followed by casual mention of human trafficking
jokes.explanation: Dark prison humor with layered meaning. 'Sleeping under me' has obvious implications, while selling for cigarettes shows how quickly Tobias lost value.

jokes.text: Whoa! Whoa! What are you folks doin' on this part of the mountain? We're lost. Well, I can give you a ride back into town. Come on. Let's go. Buck, Banjo, get on up here. Come on, boy.
jokes.setup: Employees took wrong bus to Catalina
jokes.punchline: They ended up so lost they're being rescued by hillbillies with dogs
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of how wrong the lunch trip went. The hillbilly rescue with named dogs adds absurdist flavor to complete the employees' journey.

tmdb_overview: When Michael discovers his family has been receiving paychecks without working, he forces them all to get jobs within the company. Lindsay questions his management skills, and Tobias checks himself in at George Sr.'s prison to research a film role.
insight: Escalation humor carries 81.9 score on modest 1.94 jokes-per-minute — quality over quantity.
